params.text:
Noticing changes

Because you help a person with such close, regular care, you are often the first to notice when something changes. This makes you an important pair of eyes for the whole care team. A change may be physical, or it may be in how a person seems in themselves.

  • Skin: redness, broken skin, rashes, bruising or swelling.
  • Body: weight loss or gain, poor appetite, or new pain.
  • Mood and ability: low mood, confusion, or new difficulty with tasks.

None of these needs a diagnosis from you — your job is simply to notice and to pass it on.

Why small changes matter

A change that seems minor can be the first sign of something that needs attention — an infection, dehydration, a pressure ulcer beginning to form, or a person becoming unwell or unhappy. Someone who is normally chatty falling quiet, or who suddenly needs more help than usual, is telling you something even without words.

Because you see the person regularly, you come to know what is normal for them, and that makes you well placed to spot what is not. Trust your instinct: if something feels different or not right, it is always worth reporting. It is far better to mention a change that turns out to be nothing than to stay silent about one that mattered. Reporting early gives the team the best chance to help the person quickly.

params.question: You notice that a person who is usually cheerful and chatty has become quiet and withdrawn over a few visits. What is the right thing to do?

answers.text: Report the change so the team can look into it, even though you cannot say what is causing it

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A change in mood can be an early sign of something wrong, so report it for the team to explore.

answers.text: Say nothing, as mood is not part of personal care

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Changes in mood and manner are important and should always be noticed and reported.

answers.text: Wait until the next monthly review to mention it

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Waiting weeks could delay help; changes should be reported promptly.

answers.text: Decide it must be nothing and forget about it

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. It is not for you to dismiss a change; report it so the team can decide whether it matters.
